title: Towards Generalized Schubert Varieties and Anabelian Matroids
speaker: Mateusz Michalek
abstract:
The groundbreaking results by Huh (further extended in joint work with Adiprasito and Katz) allowed to associate to a matroid a class in the Chow ring of the permutohedral variety. The technique turned out to be especially powerful, as certain invariants of the matroid could be expressed as intersection products in this Chow ring. Can this technique be used further, beyond matroids and the permutohedral variety?
We will point out very explicit analogies of the work of Huh and Schubert. This will bring us to applications e.g. in statistics, through maximum likelihood degree. 
Finally, we will go back to combinatorics, presenting closed formulas for mixed Eulerian numbers.

proof_stages.label: Resolution of diagonal inversion by blowups
proof_stages.summary: The failed inversion map for projectivized diagonal matrices is resolved by successive blowups along lower-rank strata, producing the permutohedral variety with hyperplane divisors Li and exceptional divisors Si.

proof_stages.label: Matroidal strict transforms and mixed Eulerian intersections
proof_stages.summary: Projectivized linear spaces of diagonal matrices, viewed as matroid representations, have strict transforms in the permutohedral variety whose intersections with the Li recover chromatic-polynomial coefficients and mixed or matroidal mixed Eulerian numbers.

proof_stages.label: Symmetric-matrix generalization via complete quadrics
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by quantum and spectrahedral analogies, diagonal matrices are replaced by symmetric matrices, yielding complete quadrics and strict transforms of linear spaces whose Li-intersections include Schubert characteristic numbers, SDP degrees, and Gaussian concentration-model degrees.

proof_stages.label: Schubert’s trade from Li divisors to exceptional Si divisors
proof_stages.summary: The speaker isolates Schubert’s strategy: use the linear relations between the Li and Si divisors so that intersections with exceptional divisors decompose into smaller problems, combinatorially mirroring facets of the permutohedron as products of smaller permutohedra.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial encoding and explicit exceptional-intersection formula
proof_stages.summary: All mixed Eulerian intersections are packaged into an Li-volume polynomial and compared, by a linear change of coordinates, with the Si-intersection polynomial, for which the speaker gives a simple signed multinomial product formula.

proof_stages.label: Quantum Pascal triangle and column-polynomial phenomenon
proof_stages.summary: The complete-quadrics analogue of the binomial-coefficient triangle is introduced, with a theorem that its columns are polynomial and with open or conjectural log-concavity properties of the resulting coefficient sequences.

proof_stages.label: Matroidal extension and Anabelian-matroid program
proof_stages.summary: The exceptional-divisor method is stated to extend to matroidal mixed Eulerian numbers, while the SN-invariant intersection theory of the permutohedron is positioned as the natural source of evaluative matroid invariants and a possible definition scheme for Anabelian matroids.

examples.name: Quantum superposition versus probabilistic mixture
examples.note: The distinction between a vector superposition and a mixture encoded by rank-one or higher-rank Hermitian matrices is used to motivate the move from diagonal to symmetric matrices as the “quantum” analogue.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains the superposition/mixture distinction by passing vectors to rank-one symmetric or Hermitian matrices and comparing linear combinations before versus after that construction.

passes.reason: Pure quantum states are represented by rank-one density matrices, hence boundary/extreme points of the density-matrix cone, and the contrast is used only as motivation.
the superposition/pure-state side is represented by a rank-one density matrix, a minimal-rank extreme state rather than a counterexample
Pure quantum states are modeled by rank-one projectors, a minimal-rank boundary/extreme case of the density-matrix state space, used as motivating contrast with mixtures.
Pure superpositions are represented by rank-one density matrices, extreme boundary points of the state space, contrasted motivationally with mixed states.
pure superpositions are represented by rank-one density matrices, the minimal-rank extreme points/rays of the positive semidefinite state cone

It is used to show that treating vector superposition as mere probabilistic uncertainty fails, and pure states are rank-one extreme boundary points of the density-matrix cone.
Pure superpositions are represented by rank-one density matrices, extreme boundary points contrasted with mixed states only as motivation.
The distinction is invoked to rule out encoding ignorance by state-vector linear combinations, with pure states rank-one extreme boundary points of the density-matrix cone.
It obstructs the naive identification of uncertainty with vector linear combinations, using pure states as rank-one extremal density matrices.
Refutes the naive identification of vector superposition with classical uncertainty; pure density states are rank-one extreme boundary cases.
Pure superposed states are represented by rank-one density matrices, extreme points of the state space, and the contrast is used as motivation rather than refutation.

examples.name: Grassmannian and Lagrangian Grassmannian comparison
examples.note: An audience question compares complete collineations and complete quadrics with Grassmannian and Lagrangian Grassmannian constructions, which the speaker treats as an “almost” relation via a torus quotient.
examples.engagement_reason: The Grassmannian/Lagrangian Grassmannian comparison arises in a brief audience exchange where he states a quotient-type relation but does not compute or develop it.

passes.reason: Grassmannians are homogeneous highly symmetric spaces, and the Lagrangian Grassmannian parametrizes maximal isotropic subspaces, but the comparison is only a corrected analogy.

examples.name: Classical Eulerian numbers and ascents
examples.note: The ascent-counting interpretation and the Wikipedia alternating-sum formula for Eulerian numbers illustrate the tension between combinatorial positivity and closed formulas before passing to mixed Eulerian numbers.
examples.engagement_reason: He discusses Eulerian numbers as self-intersections/volumes, gives the ascent interpretation and the closed summation formula, and uses them to motivate mixed Eulerian formulas.

passes.reason: Classical Eulerian numbers arise from the regular ascent distribution, equivalently symmetric hypersimplex volumes, and serve as a baseline example.
